metadata.title: The Cost Inversion: Why Hiring an OPT Candidate Is Cheaper
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Reallocation Engine card 09. Source chapter: the-reallocation-engine/chapters/12-the-opt-framing-generator.md. Mechanism: FICA exemption makes OPT hire cheaper than domestic hire during OPT period.
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock

style_bible.visual_style: editorial paper collage on real newsprint scan; desaturated plates; flat annotation plane; serif labels with hairline underlines
style_bible.color_palette: cream ground, charcoal serif type, teal = OPT hire / cheaper / FICA-exempt, crimson = wrong assumption / cost-more narrative, gold highlighter as the single editor's-pen voice

metadata.color_semantics: TEAL #1F6F5C = OPT hire / cheaper / FICA-exempt / the real cost; CRIMSON #BF3339 = the wrong assumption / cost-more narrative / the filter that fires first; never swap mid-film
metadata.note: Card exclusions honored: NO immigration-law specifics about H-1B future sponsorship costs; NO detail on STEM OPT extension; NO framing strategy tiers; NO legal advice framing. Focus is purely the FICA exemption mechanism and cost comparison. Worked example numbers (salary $90,000, FICA $6,885) labeled illustrative.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: A recruiter sees 'international student' and a filter fires. Sponsorship. Cost. Legal risk. The candidate is out before the first question.

card.sub: Why Hiring an OPT Candidate Is Cheaper

beats.narration_text: An OPT candidate should cost more to hire — visa overhead, legal complexity, future risk. Here is the case where they cost less. Why?

card.copy: An OPT candidate should cost more to hire. Here is the case where they cost less.

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] THE QUESTION — gap formula on screen

beats.narration_text: The assumption is reasonable. International hires sound expensive: visa filings, attorney fees, sponsorship timelines. So the filter fires before the math runs.

production_viz.label: the cost-more assumption
production_viz.mechanic: a crimson label chip 'INTERNATIONAL' slides in; below it, three crimson cost-items appear one by one: 'visa filings', 'attorney fees', 'sponsorship risk'; a horizontal crimson bar grows to the right labeled 'assumed cost penalty'
production_viz.note: the naive expectation — cost penalty assumed without checking

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] the assumed cost items stack up in crimson

beats.narration_text: But there is a fact that cuts the other way. Students on F-1 OPT are generally exempt from FICA taxes — Social Security and Medicare — for the OPT period. So is the employer.

production_viz.label: FICA exemption — both sides
production_viz.mechanic: two chips appear side by side: 'EMPLOYEE' (teal) and 'EMPLOYER' (teal); below each, the FICA line 'Social Security + Medicare' appears; both get a teal bracket labeled 'EXEMPT during OPT'
production_viz.note: the mechanism — exemption applies to BOTH employee and employer

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] FICA exempt — employee AND employer chips

beats.narration_text: The employer match is roughly seven and a half percent of wages. On a domestic hire, that goes to the government. On an OPT hire, it does not leave the company.

production_viz.label: two cost bars: domestic vs OPT
production_viz.mechanic: two bars grow upward from a shared zero baseline — left bar labeled 'DOMESTIC HIRE' (crimson), right bar labeled 'OPT HIRE' (teal); the teal bar stops shorter; a gold bracket labels the delta '~7.5% FICA match'; a serif annotation below reads 'employer saves'
production_viz.note: the compare move — the visual object described on the card

viz.label: employer cost: domestic vs OPT
viz.note: proportional geometry only — no specific dollar figure in this beat

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] two cost bars — OPT bar shorter by FICA delta

beats.narration_text: The filter that fired to protect the company from extra cost was protecting it from a saving. The assumption is not just wrong — it is backwards.

production_viz.label: the backwards assumption
production_viz.mechanic: the crimson 'assumed cost penalty' bar from B03 reappears; it shrinks and reverses direction — the bar now points left, becoming teal; a serif label updates from 'ASSUMED COST PENALTY' to 'ACTUAL SAVING'
production_viz.note: the implication — the direction is inverted

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] crimson assumption bar reverses into teal saving

beats.narration_text: Illustrative: a ninety-thousand-dollar salary. A domestic hire costs the employer sixty-eight hundred and eighty-five dollars in FICA match. An OPT hire costs zero — for that period. The screen that filtered them out cost the company the saving.

production_viz.label: illustrative example: $90k salary
production_viz.mechanic: two side-by-side panels; left (crimson): 'DOMESTIC HIRE / $90,000 salary / FICA match: $6,885'; right (teal): 'OPT HIRE / $90,000 salary / FICA match: $0'; a teal chip below right panel reads 'SAVES $6,885'; a small serif note 'illustrative' floats beneath both panels
production_viz.note: figures labeled illustrative — from card seed. No legal or H-1B content.

viz.label: illustrative example
viz.note: illustrative — labeled as such on screen

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] side-by-side example panels with dollar figures

beats.narration_text: An OPT candidate should cost more. The FICA exemption makes them cheaper. The filter fired on a wrong assumption — in the direction that cost the candidate the role.

card.copy: OPT hire assumed to cost more. FICA exemption makes them cheaper.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the The Reallocation Engine series.
